examples.q: A force of 50 N is applied to a small piston with an area of 0.01 m². What is the pressure transmitted through the fluid?
examples.solution:
Pascal's Principle states that pressure applied to
an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally and
undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid.

First, calculate the pressure using:
    P = F / A

where  P = pressure  (Pa — Pascals)
       F = force     (N)
       A = area      (m²)

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    F = 50 N
    A = 0.01 m²

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    P = 50 / 0.01
    P = 5 000 Pa

Answer:  P = 5 000 Pa

This pressure is transmitted equally throughout
the entire enclosed fluid.

examples.q: A hydraulic lift has a small piston of area 0.02 m² and a large piston of area 0.20 m². A force of 100 N is applied to the small piston. What force does the large piston exert?
examples.solution:
By Pascal's Principle, the pressure is the same
at both pistons in a hydraulic system:

    P₁ = P₂
    F₁ / A₁ = F₂ / A₂

Rearranging to find F₂:
    F₂ = F₁ × (A₂ / A₁)

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    F₁ = 100 N
    A₁ = 0.02 m²
    A₂ = 0.20 m²

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    F₂ = 100 × (0.20 / 0.02)
    F₂ = 100 × 10
    F₂ = 1 000 N

Answer:  F₂ = 1 000 N

The hydraulic system multiplies the force by 10×
because the area ratio is 10:1.

examples.q: In a hydraulic system, piston A has area 0.05 m² and piston B has area 0.5 m². If piston A moves down 10 cm, how far does piston B move up?
examples.solution:
In a hydraulic system, the volume of fluid
displaced is the same on both sides:

    V₁ = V₂
    A₁ × d₁ = A₂ × d₂

Rearranging to find d₂:
    d₂ = (A₁ × d₁) / A₂

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    A₁ = 0.05 m²
    d₁ = 10 cm = 0.10 m
    A₂ = 0.5 m²

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    d₂ = (0.05 × 0.10) / 0.5
    d₂ = 0.005 / 0.5
    d₂ = 0.01 m  =  1 cm

Answer:  Piston B rises  1 cm

Note: The large piston moves a SMALLER distance.
Force is gained but distance is sacrificed.

questions.given:
This principle applies to enclosed, incompressible fluids.
It is the basis of all hydraulic systems.
opts: Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally in all directions
Pressure decreases with depth in a fluid
A fluid in motion exerts less pressure than a fluid at rest
The buoyant force equals the weight of fluid displaced
questions.sol: Pascal's Principle: pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished throughout the fluid in all directions.

questions.q: A force of 200 N acts on a piston with area 0.04 m². What is the pressure transmitted to the fluid?
A hydraulic system has pistons with areas 0.01 m² and 0.05 m². A 60 N force is applied to the small piston. What force acts on the large piston?
questions.given:
Pascal's Principle:  F₁ / A₁ = F₂ / A₂
→  F₂ = F₁ × (A₂ / A₁)

questions.q: In a hydraulic press, the input piston has area 0.1 m² and the output piston has area 1.0 m². What is the mechanical advantage of this system?
questions.given:
Mechanical Advantage (MA) of a hydraulic system:
    MA = A₂ / A₁  (output area / input area)
questions.sol: MA = A₂ / A₁ = 1.0 / 0.1 = 10. The system multiplies force by 10.

questions.q: A hydraulic lift must support a 9 000 N car using a large piston of area 0.9 m². What pressure must the fluid exert?
If the pressure in a hydraulic system is 8 000 Pa and the output piston has area 0.25 m², what is the output force?
questions.given:
Rearrange:  P = F / A
→  F = P × A

questions.q: Which of the following is a real-world application of Pascal's Principle?
questions.given:
Pascal's Principle explains how force is multiplied
using an enclosed fluid in pistons of different sizes.
questions.sol: Hydraulic car brakes use Pascal's Principle — a small force on the brake pedal creates a large braking force at the wheels.

questions.q: Small piston: F₁ = 40 N, A₁ = 0.008 m². Large piston: A₂ = 0.04 m². Find F₂.
questions.given:
Pascal's Principle:  F₁ / A₁ = F₂ / A₂
→  F₂ = F₁ × (A₂ / A₁)

questions.q: Piston A (area = 0.02 m²) is pushed down 0.5 m. Piston B has area 0.10 m². How far does piston B move?
questions.given:
Volume conservation:  A₁ × d₁ = A₂ × d₂
→  d₂ = (A₁ × d₁) / A₂

questions.q: Why can a small force on a hydraulic brake pedal stop a large, heavy vehicle?
questions.given:
Consider Pascal's Principle and the area ratio
between the brake pedal piston and the brake pad pistons.
opts: The small input piston has much less area than the large output pistons, so pressure is multiplied into a larger force
Brakes use friction alone — fluid pressure is not involved
The fluid absorbs the force and stores it
A small force always stops large objects in hydraulics
questions.sol: Pascal's Principle transmits the same pressure to larger output pistons. F₂ = P × A₂ — a larger area means a larger stopping force.
